input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system described operates as a **Digital Shadow**, characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the state of an existing physical object (e.g., a manufacturing system or industrial asset) to a digital representation. It functions as a **passive entity**, collecting **contextual data traces** through aggregation and abstraction for a specific purpose, without autonomous operation or bidirectional feedback to the physical system. The system lacks active components for real-time control, optimization, or predictive adjustments, relying instead on external software systems to interpret and process incoming data streams. Key operational constraints include **no autonomous decision-making** and **no push-based data synchronization** with the physical twin, adhering strictly to a **read-only data acquisition paradigm** as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Kritzinger et al., 2021).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies and tools, including **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are employed to describe **component-connector perspectives**, including system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is enhanced using **SysML v1.7 state machines** and **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed behavior is simulated via **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]). General-purpose programming languages like **C++ and Java** are used for implementation. The system integrates **heterogeneous tools and notations** to address stakeholder diversity and complexity, with a focus on **semantic interoperability** and **data aggregation** for Digital Shadow and Digital Twin use cases. The methodology explicitly includes **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** as part of the implementation framework (as referenced in the abstract).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document focuses exclusively on a **single digital shadow instance** for the first vertical axis of the serial robot MABI MAX, without addressing multiplicities, hierarchical structures, or decentralized architectures of Digital Twin instances. The digital shadow is defined as a **passive data collector** ('an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'), lacking autonomous entities or coordination mechanisms for multiple instances. The document does not mention 1:N mappings, federated twins, or distributed architectures, nor does it specify scope-of-responsibility partitioning among potential twin instances. The modeling context (MBSE, ADLs like ACME/AADL) pertains to system design in general, not to the runtime multiplicity of Digital Twin components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s orchestration and coordination rely on **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, integrating heterogeneous models (e.g., **SysML** for architecture, **MontiArc Automaton** or **state machines in SysML v1.7** for behavior, and simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages such as **C++/Java** for detailed behavior). The system leverages **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc** to define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition, enabling modular integration. Resource management and system-wide coordination are implied through these ADLs and MBSE frameworks, which support hierarchical decomposition (e.g., **RFLP**, **OOSEM**, or **SMArDT** methodologies) to handle complexity. The document does not specify orchestration protocols (e.g., event-driven, service-oriented) but emphasizes the need for integrating diverse stakeholders and perspectives via these modeling tools.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The document specifies **MQTT** as the **data exchange protocol** for streaming sensor time-series data ('We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT') to the digital shadow. Horizontal integration is implied through the need to **process incoming data** from the physical system, as digital shadows are **passive** and require **external active components** (e.g., software systems) to interpret and act upon the data. While the document does not explicitly address **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) or integration with **other Digital Twins/enterprise systems**, it highlights the necessity of **heterogeneous model unification** (e.g., SysML, ADLs, simulation tools) to bridge stakeholder perspectives. The passive nature of digital shadows necessitates **dependency on external systems** for data interpretation, but no details on **enterprise integration patterns** (e.g., microservices, ESBs) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **computational models and data components** derived from **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structural and behavioral descriptions. The modeling process spans multiple abstraction layers—**requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**—employing methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**. For **functional and logical architecture**, **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to define **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**. Behavioral modeling is enhanced with tools such as **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines (v1.7)**. To refine behavior, these models are often combined with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., **C++/Java**). The DT system aggregates **contextual data traces** from the physical system, abstracting them for specific purposes, while **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** are integrated through these interconnected modeling approaches. Data sources include **automated one-way flows** from physical objects, processed via **active components** within the DT to enable real-time or near-real-time analysis. **Model relationships** are established through **compositional hierarchies** and **interfaces**, ensuring seamless interaction between geometric, behavioral, and analytical models (e.g., physics-based simulations, control logic, or performance metrics).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system interfaces with **physical actuators** comprising **permanent magnet synchronous motors** paired with **gearbox mechanisms** for torque transmission and gear reduction. The system supports **individual axis control**, accepting **position, speed, or acceleration inputs** via a centralized controller. Actuation ranges are dynamically adjustable, with **safety constraints** implicitly enforced through closed-loop control mechanisms. Addressing mechanisms rely on **real-time feedback loops** from sensors to ensure precise actuation alignment with digital model predictions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes **dual position encoders per drive axis**: a **motor-mounted encoder** for direct shaft measurement and a **secondary encoder** positioned post-gearbox to mitigate backlash and reduce positional uncertainty. Sensor data, including **input current and output angle**, is streamed in **real-time via MQTT** as time-series datasets. Spatial distribution ensures **high-resolution monitoring** of critical mechanical parameters, with sampling frequencies and transmission rates optimized for **low-latency feedback** to the Digital Twin’s control and modeling layers.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The provided document does not explicitly address **fidelity requirements, validation/verification (V&V) methods, uncertainty quantification, error detection/correction mechanisms, or quality assurance processes** for models or data. While MBSE methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs) are used to describe **structure and behavior**, their application to **fidelity assurance** or **V&V** is not elaborated. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** focuses on **one-way data flow** and **contextual data aggregation**, lacking explicit discussion of **data/model validation, uncertainty sources, or error correction**. No specifications for **tolerance thresholds, calibration protocols, or model accuracy metrics** are provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined by Kritzinger et al. [32]. This flow involves the **collection, aggregation, and abstraction of contextual data traces** from the physical system, which may include sensor data, operational logs, or environmental parameters. The data is transmitted without bidirectional interaction, meaning the digital shadow (or DT) does not actively push data back to the physical system. Preprocessing steps may include **data filtering, normalization, or transformation** to ensure compatibility with the digital model. The interaction is **passive** and relies on external software components (e.g., simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink [28] or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) to interpret and process the incoming data. No explicit details on **communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, or data quality assurance mechanisms** are provided in the document, though the aggregation and abstraction imply structured data handling. Integration mechanisms are not specified beyond the passive data collection framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for the Digital Shadow system is partially described through the mention of **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** as the protocol for streaming **time-series sensor data**. However, the document does not provide details on **network topology/architecture, bandwidth/latency requirements, reliability/redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols**. The system’s communication is limited to a **one-way data flow** from physical to digital, with no explicit bidirectional control or feedback loops. Thus, while MQTT is confirmed as a **communication protocol**, broader technical connection specifics (e.g., edge/cloud integration, protocol stacks, or fault tolerance) are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) or Digital Shadow (DS) in the provided context follows a structured development process, aligned with Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) principles. Key phases include:
  1. **Problem Identification**: Defining the purpose and scope of the digital representation, such as improving robot motion accuracy or detecting friction loss.
  2. **Asset and Model Analysis**: Evaluating the physical system's components, interfaces, and interconnections using architecture description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) to establish a logical and functional architecture.
  3. **Use or Creation of Calculations/Models**: Developing or integrating models (e.g., multi-physics models for joint friction identification) using tools like MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages (C++, Java) for behavioral modeling. This phase may involve state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton for dynamic behavior representation.
  4. **Data Identification**: Collecting and abstracting contextual data traces for the DS, ensuring alignment with the physical system's state. This includes aggregation and abstraction of data for specific use cases (e.g., continuous friction loss detection).
  5. **Concretization and Development**: Refining models through iterative development, ensuring they conform to the physical system's requirements and operational constraints. This may involve validation against real-world data or simulation tools.
  The lifecycle also implicitly includes **transition management** between phases, leveraging MBSE methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) to handle complexity across abstraction layers (requirements → functional → logical → technical). Governance is implied through compliance with modeling standards (e.g., SysML, UML) and integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives, though explicit governance frameworks are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) engineering and development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is enhanced with tools like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines**, often integrated with **MathWorks Simulink**, other **simulation tools** (e.g., [29], [30]), or **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., C++, Java). The process involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-notation model management** to ensure comprehensive system representation. **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to these methodologies, though explicit testing/validation approaches are not detailed. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly described in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin system generates **domain-specific insights** to support decision-making, primarily focused on **mechanical system optimization** (e.g., robotics). Key capabilities include:
  - **Multi-physics-aware identification**: Continuous derivation of joint friction parameters (e.g., friction loss detection) using aggregated data traces and models.
  - **Accuracy improvement**: Real-time adjustments to robot motion based on friction analysis, leveraging **visualization dashboards** for user interaction.
  - **Relational understanding**: Detection of causal relationships between system components (e.g., friction sources) to inform corrective actions.
  Analytics are driven by **model-based calculations** (e.g., Simulink, UML state machines) and **data aggregation** from physical assets, though explicit algorithms (e.g., machine learning, statistical methods) are not mentioned. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are not detailed, but the process implies validation through model conformance to physical behavior.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system adheres to **standardized modeling and interoperability frameworks**, including:
  - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **systems engineering modeling** across functional, logical, and physical layers.
  - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for **component-based system description**.
  - **Unified Modeling Language (UML)**: Employed in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (e.g., as a **UML class diagram**) to define conceptual structures.
  - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)**: Implemented for **standardized asset representation** in industrial contexts (though the specific **Industrial Digital Twins Association (IDTA) RAMI 4.0 framework** is not mentioned in the provided text).
  The system emphasizes **interoperability** through **multi-notation modeling** (e.g., SysML + ADLs + simulation tools) and **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., Simulink, C++, Java). **Certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks** are not explicitly detailed in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents reference safety considerations in the context of **Digital Shadows** and **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** implementations within the **Automation Pyramid** (Document [42]). Specifically, the **MABI robot** (Document [44]) features a **safety cell** that restricts the maximum working range to **2.25 meters** (as per the **MAX-100-2-25-P** model specification), ensuring physical safety during operation. However, no explicit details on **cybersecurity protocols**, **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, or **compliance with safety standards** (e.g., IEC 61508, IEC 62061) are provided in the documents. The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (Documents [46], [47], [50]) is mentioned as a structural component for digital twin integration but lacks specific technical descriptions of **safety-related submodels** or **risk assessment methodologies**.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document,' but the source text explicitly details the system under study as the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, including its components and operational domain. Since evidence exists in the text, the claim that it is missing is incorrect.

The description accurately captures the definition and behavior of the Digital Shadow as presented in the text. It correctly identifies it as a 'passive entity' with an 'automated one-way data flow' that does not 'pushes data back to the physical system'. It also correctly lists the services provided, such as collecting 'contextual data traces' and enabling 'semantic support' and 'understanding of the physical relationships' through the linking of data and models.

The extraction comprehensively lists the tools, languages, and frameworks mentioned in the background section, including SysML, various ADLs, Simulink, and AAS. It correctly identifies their roles in multi-level abstraction and simulation.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
